stem: You are the obstetric medicine registrar reviewing a 33-year-old G2P1 non-Aboriginal woman at 26 weeks gestation with acute onset pleuritic chest pain and breathlessness for 6 hours. She has no haemoptysis and no calf swelling on examination. She has no past venous thromboembolism but had a long-haul flight returning to Australia three weeks ago. Her chest x-ray is normal. She is haemodynamically stable.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 112, BP 118/72, RR 22, SpO2 95% room air
data_table.Examination: Clear chest, no calf tenderness or asymmetry, fetal heart heard, soft non-tender abdomen
data_table.Investigations: Chest x-ray clear, ECG sinus tachycardia, troponin normal, FBE normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: D-dimer level interpreted using an age-adjusted threshold to decide whether further imaging is required for diagnostic confirmation
options.rationale: D-dimer is physiologically elevated throughout pregnancy and cannot be used to rule out venous thromboembolism in pregnant women. (Source: RANZCOG VTE in Pregnancy Statement)

options.text: Empirical therapeutic enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg subcutaneous daily without imaging because pregnancy itself precludes further radiological investigation
options.rationale: Imaging confirmation is required because anticoagulating without diagnosis exposes mother and fetus to bleeding risk during a 6 month course; therapeutic enoxaparin is commenced while awaiting imaging. (Source: RANZCOG VTE in Pregnancy Statement)

options.text: Bilateral lower limb compression ultrasound first, and if positive for proximal deep venous thrombosis treat without further chest imaging being required
options.rationale: Australian guidance recommends bilateral leg ultrasound first because a positive proximal DVT confirms the indication for anticoagulation and avoids fetal radiation exposure from CTPA or V/Q. (Source: RANZCOG VTE in Pregnancy Statement)

options.text: Computed tomography pulmonary angiogram as first-line imaging because it has higher sensitivity than ventilation-perfusion scan for peripheral pulmonary embolism
options.rationale: CTPA delivers higher breast radiation than V/Q and is not preferred first line unless chest x-ray is abnormal; in pregnancy with normal chest x-ray, V/Q is favoured if leg ultrasound is negative. (Source: RANZCOG VTE in Pregnancy Statement)

options.text: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging to assess right ventricular strain and exclude pulmonary embolism without ionising radiation exposure to the developing fetus
options.rationale: Cardiac MRI does not reliably image the pulmonary vasculature to diagnose or exclude pulmonary embolism and is not part of the diagnostic pathway. (Source: RANZCOG VTE in Pregnancy Statement)

explanation.summary: Suspected pulmonary embolism in pregnancy requires a modified diagnostic pathway because D-dimer is unreliable. Bilateral lower limb compression ultrasound is performed first; a positive proximal DVT obviates the need for further chest imaging. If ultrasound is negative, ventilation-perfusion scan is preferred over CTPA when the chest x-ray is normal because of lower maternal breast radiation, while CTPA is preferred when chest x-ray is abnormal. Therapeutic dose low-molecular-weight heparin is started while awaiting imaging.
key_points: D-dimer is not reliable in pregnancy
Bilateral leg ultrasound first; positive proximal DVT means no chest imaging needed
V/Q preferred over CTPA when CXR normal (lower breast radiation)
CTPA preferred if CXR abnormal because V/Q less interpretable
Therapeutic LMWH commenced while awaiting confirmation
explanation.pearls: Enoxaparin in pregnancy is dosed by booking weight, typically 1 mg/kg twice daily for treatment, with anti-Xa monitoring not routinely required.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar at a tertiary unit reviewing a 36-year-old G4P3 non-Aboriginal woman at 32 weeks gestation. She has had three previous caesarean sections. Anterior placenta praevia was confirmed at the 20 week morphology scan and a recent dedicated tertiary ultrasound documents bridging vessels and bladder wall interruption suggestive of placenta accreta spectrum. She has had no antepartum bleeding and the fetus is appropriately grown. Her haemoglobin is 118 g/L.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, fundal height appropriate, fetal heart present, no per-vaginal bleed
data_table.Investigations: Hb 118 g/L, ferritin 35 micrograms/L, blood group A positive, antibody screen negative; ultrasound features suspicious for placenta accreta spectrum

lead_in: What is the most appropriate delivery plan?

options.text: Trial of labour after caesarean at 39 weeks gestation with continuous CTG monitoring and immediate caesarean only if vaginal bleeding occurs in early labour
options.rationale: Trial of labour is absolutely contraindicated with placenta praevia and accreta spectrum because of catastrophic haemorrhage risk; planned operative delivery is mandatory. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Accreta Statement)

options.text: Planned MDT caesarean at 34 to 36 weeks after corticosteroids, with consultant obstetrician, urology, anaesthetics, blood bank cross-match and consideration of leaving placenta in situ
options.rationale: Australian guidance recommends planned multidisciplinary caesarean at 34 to 36 weeks with corticosteroids, blood products on standby, urology support, and consideration of caesarean hysterectomy versus leaving the placenta in situ based on intra-operative findings. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Accreta Statement)

options.text: Defer planning until 39 weeks gestation to maximise fetal lung maturity, then perform standard elective caesarean section under spinal anaesthesia at the regional hospital
options.rationale: Deferring to 39 weeks risks emergency presentation with massive haemorrhage; tertiary planned delivery at 34 to 36 weeks with full team and blood products is required. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Accreta Statement)

options.text: External cephalic version followed by induction of labour at 38 weeks with epidural analgesia and active management of third stage with intramuscular oxytocin
options.rationale: ECV and vaginal birth are contraindicated with placenta praevia and accreta spectrum because of catastrophic haemorrhage risk; planned caesarean is the only safe route. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Accreta Statement)

options.text: Elective caesarean at 37 weeks at the local district hospital under general anaesthesia without prior MDT planning, with on-call urology consulted only if bladder injury occurs
options.rationale: Care must be at a tertiary unit with prospective MDT involvement; reactive specialist involvement after injury misses the point of planned coordinated care. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Accreta Statement)

explanation.summary: Placenta accreta spectrum is the leading cause of obstetric hysterectomy and major haemorrhage. Risk rises sharply with each previous caesarean particularly in the setting of placenta praevia. Australian guidance mandates prospective tertiary multidisciplinary care including obstetrics, anaesthetics, urology, interventional radiology, neonatal intensive care, haematology and blood bank. Planned caesarean at 34 to 36 weeks after corticosteroids balances prematurity against the risk of unscheduled bleeding. Options include caesarean hysterectomy or leaving the placenta in situ followed by methotrexate or delayed hysterectomy.
key_points: Prior caesarean plus placenta praevia is the strongest risk factor
Planned delivery at 34 to 36 weeks after corticosteroids in a tertiary unit
Multidisciplinary planning: obstetrics, urology, anaesthetics, blood bank, NICU
Vaginal birth and trial of labour are absolutely contraindicated
Options include hysterectomy or leaving placenta in situ
explanation.pearls: The risk of placenta accreta with placenta praevia rises from around 3% with no previous caesarean to over 60% with three or more previous caesareans.

stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 15-year-old non-Aboriginal girl with a four month history of persistent low mood, anhedonia, fatigue and reduced school attendance. She has had no manic features. She has been engaged in weekly trauma-focused cognitive behavioural therapy for eight weeks without improvement. She denies active suicidal ideation but has fleeting passive thoughts of being better off dead. Her parents are present and supportive of medication.

data_table.Examination: Mood low, affect restricted, no psychomotor agitation, thought form linear, no perceptual disturbance, denies plan or intent
data_table.Investigations: TSH normal, FBE normal, ferritin normal, urine pregnancy test negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate pharmacological choice?

options.text: Sertraline 25 mg orally daily titrated to 50 mg after 1 week with weekly clinical review for 4 weeks and shared safety planning
options.rationale: Sertraline is not the first-line antidepressant for adolescents in Australia because evidence for efficacy in this age group is strongest for fluoxetine, which is the recommended initial choice. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Mirtazapine 15 mg orally at night titrated weekly with regular face-to-face review and explicit safety planning that includes a written crisis plan
options.rationale: Mirtazapine is not recommended first line in adolescents; the strongest randomised evidence in this age group supports fluoxetine, which is preferred. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Continue psychological therapy alone for a further 8 weeks before reconsidering pharmacotherapy because medication carries a suicidality warning in adolescents
options.rationale: Failure of an adequate trial of psychological therapy in moderate adolescent depression is an indication to add pharmacotherapy; delay risks ongoing functional impairment. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Venlafaxine 37.5 mg orally daily titrated weekly with face-to-face review at one week and explicit safety planning agreed with family and patient
options.rationale: Venlafaxine is not first line in adolescents because of higher rates of suicidality and discontinuation syndrome compared with fluoxetine. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Fluoxetine 10 mg orally daily increased to 20 mg after one week, weekly clinical review for four weeks with explicit safety planning and continued psychological therapy
options.rationale: Fluoxetine is the first-line antidepressant for adolescent depression in Australia because it has the strongest evidence in this age group; weekly review covers the early increase in suicidality risk. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

explanation.summary: Australian RANZCP guidance recommends fluoxetine as the first-line antidepressant in adolescents because it has the strongest randomised controlled trial evidence in this age group, in particular the TADS trial. Combination therapy with cognitive behavioural therapy is more effective than either modality alone. Weekly face-to-face review is recommended for the first 4 weeks because of a small absolute increase in suicidality in the early weeks of treatment. A written safety plan and engagement of family or carers are integral to safe prescribing in this age group.
key_points: Fluoxetine is first-line antidepressant in adolescents
Combine pharmacotherapy with CBT for best outcomes
Weekly face-to-face review for first 4 weeks to monitor suicidality
Engage family and document a written safety plan
Other SSRIs and venlafaxine are second line because of evidence gaps and side effects
explanation.pearls: Fluoxetine 20 mg daily is the target dose in adolescents with response typically assessed at 4 to 6 weeks before considering increase or switch.

stem: You are the psychiatry registrar reviewing a 28-year-old non-Aboriginal woman with bipolar I disorder, currently euthymic six weeks after a manic episode requiring brief admission. She has been stable on quetiapine 600 mg daily but has gained 7 kg. She and her consultant agree to switch to lithium for relapse prevention. She is in a relationship and has had a Mirena IUD inserted last year. She has no past renal, thyroid or cardiac disease.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic, no abnormal movements, thyroid not palpably enlarged
data_table.Investigations: U&E pending, TSH pending, calcium pending, ECG pending, beta-hCG today negative

lead_in: Which baseline workup is required before commencing lithium?

options.text: Full blood examination, liver function tests and serum prolactin level before commencing lithium therapy at an initial dose of 250 mg twice daily
options.rationale: FBE, LFTs and prolactin are not the core baseline panel for lithium; the relevant tests are urea and electrolytes, thyroid function, calcium, ECG, weight and a pregnancy test. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Urea, electrolytes and creatinine; thyroid function; serum calcium; ECG; weight and a pregnancy test before commencing lithium at an initial low dose
options.rationale: Lithium baseline panel covers renal function, thyroid function, calcium, ECG and pregnancy status because of long-term renal, thyroid, parathyroid, cardiac and teratogenic risks. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Urea, electrolytes and creatinine alone without other tests because lithium's main adverse effect is nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and reduced glomerular filtration rate
options.rationale: Renal function alone misses thyroid and parathyroid screening required because of hypothyroidism and hyperparathyroidism risks, and pregnancy must be excluded because of Ebstein anomaly risk. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Echocardiogram and exercise stress test to assess cardiac function before commencing lithium because of conduction abnormalities and risk of cardiomyopathy progression
options.rationale: Echocardiography and stress testing are not part of routine lithium initiation; a baseline 12-lead ECG suffices in patients without cardiac history. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: MRI brain and electroencephalogram to assess baseline neurological status before commencing lithium because of neurotoxicity risk and tremor reporting in many users
options.rationale: Neuroimaging and EEG are not part of routine lithium initiation; baseline neurological screening is clinical only and no imaging is required. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

explanation.summary: Lithium remains a first-line mood stabiliser for bipolar I relapse prevention. Baseline workup before initiation includes renal function (U&E and creatinine), thyroid function, calcium, ECG, weight and a pregnancy test in women of reproductive potential because lithium is teratogenic (Ebstein anomaly). Ongoing monitoring includes serum lithium level 5 to 7 days after each dose change, then every 3 months, and 6-monthly U&E plus thyroid plus calcium with annual eGFR thereafter. The narrow therapeutic window (0.6 to 1.0 mmol/L for maintenance) requires patient education about dehydration, NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors and diuretics.
key_points: Baseline: U&E, TFT, calcium, ECG, weight, pregnancy test
Ongoing: 3-monthly level, 6-monthly U&E plus TFT plus calcium
Therapeutic range 0.6 to 1.0 mmol/L maintenance, 0.4 to 0.8 mmol/L in older adults
Avoid in early pregnancy because of Ebstein anomaly
Counsel on dehydration, NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors and thiazides as toxicity precipitants
explanation.pearls: Lithium levels should be taken 12 hours post-dose (trough), with target 0.6 to 1.0 mmol/L for maintenance and 0.4 to 0.8 mmol/L in adults over 65 years.

stem: You are the addiction medicine registrar reviewing a 48-year-old non-Aboriginal man on day one of planned community alcohol detoxification. He has been drinking 200 g alcohol daily for 10 years. He has had two previous unprovoked alcohol withdrawal seizures. He has no liver decompensation and is keen to remain at home with carer support. His last drink was 12 hours ago and he is mildly tremulous but oriented.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 96, BP 142/86, RR 18, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: Mild tremor, anxious, no diaphoresis, oriented to time and place, no asterixis, no jaundice
data_table.Investigations: LFTs mildly deranged GGT 240 U/L, ALT 62, AST 70, INR 1.0, FBE normal, BSL 5.4 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate prescription?

options.text: Oral thiamine 300 mg daily and outpatient detox without sedative cover because his withdrawal severity is currently mild and he has carer supervision at home
options.rationale: A history of two previous withdrawal seizures makes seizure recurrence likely without benzodiazepine cover; thiamine alone is insufficient for prophylaxis. (Source: RACGP Alcohol Withdrawal Guidelines)

options.text: Oral diazepam loading 20 mg every 2 hours symptom-triggered using CIWA-Ar scoring with thiamine cover and admission to hospital because of his seizure history
options.rationale: Previous withdrawal seizures and high daily intake mandate inpatient detox with diazepam symptom-triggered dosing plus parenteral thiamine cover for Wernicke prevention. (Source: RACGP Alcohol Withdrawal Guidelines)

options.text: Oral lorazepam 1 mg every 6 hours for 5 days at home with general practitioner review every second day and thiamine 100 mg orally daily as outpatient support
options.rationale: Lorazepam at this dose is unlikely to control withdrawal in heavy drinkers with previous seizures, and outpatient management is unsafe in this risk profile. (Source: RACGP Alcohol Withdrawal Guidelines)

options.text: Oral carbamazepine 200 mg three times daily as primary withdrawal therapy and outpatient detoxification with daily clinic review and oral thiamine supplementation
options.rationale: Carbamazepine is occasionally used in mild withdrawal but is not first line in Australia and is inadequate alone for heavy drinkers with a seizure history. (Source: RACGP Alcohol Withdrawal Guidelines)

options.text: Oral chlordiazepoxide 25 mg every 6 hours fixed-schedule for 5 days at home with no thiamine because of his preserved nutritional status and normal full blood count
options.rationale: Outpatient fixed-schedule benzodiazepine in a patient with previous withdrawal seizures and heavy intake is unsafe, and thiamine cover is required regardless of nutritional appearance. (Source: RACGP Alcohol Withdrawal Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Alcohol withdrawal severity is predicted by daily intake, duration, previous withdrawal complications and concurrent illness. Patients with previous withdrawal seizures, delirium tremens, intake above 150 g daily, significant comorbidity or unstable home environment warrant inpatient detoxification with symptom-triggered diazepam (loaded 20 mg every 2 hours by CIWA-Ar score) and parenteral thiamine cover. Thiamine 300 mg intramuscular or intravenous daily for at least 3 days precedes any glucose-containing fluid to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy. Long-acting benzodiazepines are preferred for smoother withdrawal except in significant hepatic impairment where lorazepam or oxazepam are used.
key_points: Previous withdrawal seizures mandate inpatient detoxification with benzodiazepine cover
Diazepam 20 mg loading every 2 hours symptom-triggered is the Australian standard
Parenteral thiamine before any glucose-containing fluid to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy
Lorazepam or oxazepam in significant hepatic impairment because they avoid hepatic oxidation
CIWA-Ar score guides ongoing dosing
explanation.pearls: Parenteral thiamine 300 mg intramuscular daily for at least 3 days is given before any intravenous glucose to prevent precipitating Wernicke encephalopathy in chronic alcohol use.
